posts.title: On Intentional Camera Movement: Surrendering Control
posts.excerpt: There is a particular kind of terror in letting go of a photograph mid-exposure. ICM asks you to unlearn everything you know about holding still.
posts.body:
There is a particular kind of terror in letting go of a photograph mid-exposure. ICM asks you to unlearn everything you know about holding still. The wrist rotation that destroys a landscape becomes the gesture that saves it.

I've been practising for six years and I still don't fully know what I'm doing — and that's exactly the point. The moment you think you've mastered it, the results start feeling mechanical. ICM requires a commitment to genuine uncertainty.

The mechanics

A shutter speed between 1/4 and 2 seconds is where most of the interesting work happens. Too fast and the blur is too subtle. Too slow and the image dissolves into abstraction — which can be beautiful, but it's a different discipline.

The camera is just a brush. The sensor is just a canvas. The motion is the paint.

I hold the camera loosely — no death grip, no locked wrists. I let the weight of the lens do part of the work. For vertical blurs like forest trees, a slow upward motion with a slight spiral at the end. For horizontal urban blurs, a side-to-side motion with a late acceleration.

What you can't control

Wind. Your own heartbeat at 1/2 second. The subject moving independently of your motion. These are features, not bugs. The images that surprise me most are always the ones where something moved that I didn't intend.
posts.metaDescription: An exploration of intentional camera movement — how surrendering control of your camera can produce the most expressive images.

posts.title: Why I Stopped Shooting Digital Entirely
posts.excerpt: The decision took three years to make and about ten minutes to commit to. Film forces decisions. Digital defers them.
posts.body:
The decision took three years to make and about ten minutes to commit to. I'd been shooting hybrid — film for personal work, digital for commissions — and the gap between the two was becoming embarrassing. Not in quality. In intention.

Digital is a medium of infinite takes. Film is a medium of considered ones. My digital work was technically fine and completely bloodless. My film work had accidents in it, and the accidents were the best parts.

The cost of infinite storage

When storage is free, editing is deferred. I would come home from a shoot with 800 RAW files and spend a week in Lightroom making choices I should have made in the field. Film made me make those choices before I pressed the shutter.

You can't bracket your way to a good photograph. You can only decide.

I'm not arguing that film is objectively better. I'm arguing that the constraints of film made me a better photographer. The 36-exposure limit. The cost per frame. The wait for development. All of it forces clarity.
posts.metaDescription: Why Anna Voss chose to shoot exclusively on film — and what the constraint of 36 exposures taught her about intention.

posts.title: Printing on Fibre: A Slower Practice
posts.excerpt: Resin-coated paper dries in minutes. Fibre takes hours. That difference in time changes how you think.
posts.body:
Resin-coated paper dries in minutes. Fibre takes hours. That difference in time changes how you think about a print.

With RC paper, you can run a test strip, adjust, and have a finished print in twenty minutes. With fibre, the drying, flattening, and washing alone take the better part of a day. You make fewer prints. You consider each one more carefully.

Why bother

Permanence. A well-processed fibre print, properly fixed and washed, will outlast any inkjet or digital print made today. Archivally speaking, we're talking centuries versus decades. For work that matters, that matters.

There's also the surface. Fibre paper has a depth that RC can't replicate — the highlights have a warmth, the shadows have a density. Under a loupe you can see the silver in the emulsion. You're looking at actual silver, not a dye simulation of it.

Every fibre print is an object. An RC print is a document.

My process: expose, develop in Ilford Multigrade, stop, fix, wash for ten minutes, tone in selenium 1:20 for four minutes, wash for forty minutes, dry face-down on a mesh rack overnight. No shortcuts.
posts.metaDescription: The slow, deliberate practice of printing on fibre-based paper in the darkroom — and why the extra time is worth it.
